item.title: Formally perfect.
item.content:encoded:
The item per se, i thought, is nothing special, but the precision in the description of testing protocol is briliant and the final addendum is amazing.
1184 could help "resolving" IDAS, probably (or create the most intricate paradox ever)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice existential horror at the end. I liked this one.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, it was good until the last paragraph. "It's all just a dream" or "it's all just an illusion" is a horrible, lazy writing excuse, even when the dream or illusion is horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This description is so logical.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

Out of curiosity, would you care to explain what's behind the REDACTED sections? They're used to decent effect in the article (even if a bit excessively), but I feel like I don't have the whole story, and I'd at least like to know.

item.title: Re: Tag as Cognitohazard
item.content:encoded: It doesn't matter what becomes the auditory cognitohazard, it's part of the SCPs core anomalous effect and thus needs the cognitohazard and auditory tags.

item.title: Re: Tag as Cognitohazard
item.content:encoded:
I have to respectfully disagree, because the tag is defined as

A cognitohazard is something that poses a danger to any subject that perceives it with any of our five physical senses

By wearing the ring, the user's voice becomes an auditory cognitohazard with mind-affecting properties. Seeing the ring, touching it, etc. poses no danger; note that there were no sensory tags (visual, auditory) added to this prior.

That is, unless I missed something in the article. If I'm wrong, apologies for preemptively removing the tag before discussing it.

item.title: Re: Tag as Cognitohazard
item.content:encoded:
That doesn't mean you should then just go ahead and add the tag. We have a technical team in charge of tagging articles. If you feel a tag should be added or removed for any reason, get in touch with them first.

That said, you're right in this case, as the SCP causes an effect that uses an auditory vector to affect subjects.

item.title: Tag as Cognitohazard
item.content:encoded: Due to this SCP's ability to completely change someone's point of view and the ending statement of the SCP page, I think SCP-1184 should be tagged as a Cognitohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dat Incident Followup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last bit is so sad. They know that their whole lives are likely lies at this point …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it, but also I think it's a terrible idea to restrict the use of an inherently-compromising artifact to highly-trusted-to-not-be-compromised personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am upvoting entirely because of 'Incident-1184-7 Followup.'
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2+2=5

It's not easy to become sane, Winston.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last last addendum…chilling. Take my vote, dammit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I like to imagine with mind-effecting "ooo scary" type things is:

How would Sandor Clegane react?

Not amused
– hide block

I saw the last note coming, but the execution for it was terrible. I've seen the "is Foundation real or has everything we've read thus far been a lie fed to us?" thing executed far more brilliantly in a draft before. Here, it's told, not shown, and done so with brute force and no subtlety at all. It's less like planting seeds of doubt to turn people against one another, and more like just headbutting everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The implications of this are truly horrifying. +1'd heartily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last note really does it for me, as it seems many other readers. My only thoughts are that it seems like it was a long read down to that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea, mostly-well-written article, final note was a perfect "OMG!"-inducer…too many [REDACTED]s. Way too many [REDACTED]s. [REDACTED]s that make you go "Oh god, what could it be? My imagination is scaring me right now!" are good; [REDACTED]s that make you go "It looks like the author's trying way too hard to make this thing scary or mysterious" are bad. These [REDACTED]s, for me at least, fall firmly into the latter category. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last note…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that's how you do a magic item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty cliche idea, executed brilliantly. +1.
This, my friends, is how you write a high-powered SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, no vote from me. I liked loved the last note but it wasn't enough for me to give it a definite upvote. There's just something that doesn't sit right for me (particularly the comment about D-Class personnel). It isn't bad because it is well-written but… eh. Just not for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. I'm not normally a fan of DATA EXPUNGED, but it's used to great effect here. I love dangerous items that don't necessarily result in blood blood everywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The central concept is very boring, but the article is incredibly well done. Upvoted hard by the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the idea that this ring has the ability to make people do physically impossible things the second most interesting thing here, after the addendum's implications.

If this ring can make people take physical actions that they normally cannot, how far can this be taken? Could you, for example, make someone's heart stop beating, or have their bodies regenerate cells/grow new ones?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Smapti, you're an exceptional writer, but I'll have to disagree with you on this particular subject.

Perception is your own personal reality. The Foundation researchers took every precaution they could possibly take, but given the fragility of the human mind (and the very weak link it has on physical truth of reality), it is impossible to know for sure. But why challenge that beautiful, final hanging uncertainty? There's no need for closure here.

The article would paint a sense of infallibility upon the Foundation if they had the object perfectly contained… but that would be boring. No organization or individual is infallible. The follow-up is perfect because it underscores this fact very pleasantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted around the "affected people didn't BREATHE" bit, which was honestly one of the first things I was thinking.

Then that addendum, of which I have never seen such a perfect summation of "fridge horror", ACTUALLY made me try and upvote again.

I like the whole blasted thing, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a lot. I think the testing procedures (as they are as of this comment) are really well thought out and I'm glad you made the addendum collapsible. My only criticism, and it's a minor one, is that the phrase "reached Foundation ears" seems too casual for an SCP report. From the comments, it seems that was a bit of an issue in several spots, so I think you've done a nice job cleaning it up overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It takes out a lot of the mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can't even tell the difference between a banana and a ring. What makes you think you can tell who has been exposed or not? For all you know the O5s themselves have been exposed, or there might not even *be* any O5s.

Look, obviously the researchers have done what they can. They believe they have taken all of the right steps. The problem is that cant know with 100% certainty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effects of the One Ring only affect people who saw or heard the Ringbearer talking about it.

Therefore, if you assign a team of people who have not been present for its handling to verify its containment, they will not experience any effects from the cognitohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This seemingly-ordinary banana is actually SCP-1184."

You can go ask anyone you like whether the banana is in fact SCP-1184. No matter what they tell you, they will be unable to convince you that it is not. I could even add "If you ask anyone whether this banana is in fact SCP-1184, their answer always means 'yes'", and then you wouldn't even be able to understand that they were in fact telling you "no" in the first place.

Or I could just say "You have asked many people to confirm this fact, and they have all done so", and you wouldn't bother actually asking in the first place. You've already done so, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Show SCP-1184 to a person who has not previously physically examined it or met any person who has handled it.

2) Ask them, "Is this SCP-1184?"

If yes, then no containment breach has occurred.

If no, apprehend previous handlers and reacquire SCP-1184.

Problem solved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But would they have gotten a chance to prevent that from occurring? Maybe some scientist exchanged it for another ring during initial testing, before such strict containment procedures were put in. No-one would never know. Maybe the Foundation never had it at all, and someone out there is running about completely unstoppable. No-one would never know.

Also, if the entire world in its present state doesn't exist and we're actually all in a Matrix controlled by the bearer of this ring, I think it's a bit harsh to say that the Foundation is incompetent for not having stopped that. It could have happened centuries ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. This is a lot like SCP-661 , only worse, and in fact, even more like my first SCP which was downvoted due to the same reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that's what makes it lolfoundation- the Foundation would not allow a scenario to occur where the One Ring ends up in the hands of someone who would use it to manipulate the minds of Foundation staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, the addendum is quite logical. Given the established nature of the ring, you could take off your own ring, keep wearing the Veritas, and tell everyone "SCP-1184 has been returned to containment." Since the properties of the ring are already known, any and all researchers involved would perceive the effects, regardless of whether they were actually happening. Add a few more "commands", such as "The effects of the ring have been independently verified", and you can pretty much take over the Foundation. Remember how far the last known security breach got…

BTW, the implicit pun is nice. You wear it, and whatever you say has "the ring of truth". (Heh-heh.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Already liked the idea, and the execution, and the followup clinched it. Upvoted, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The addendum makes the entire Foundation look like incompetants who would allow themselves to be affected by a cognitohazard and who would be unable to distinguish reality from one, and that's really damn lolfoundation.

Aside from that, this is basically just an SCP-ified version of the One Ring, and "what if the Foundation is just something this ring made us believe in" is about as thought-provoking an idea as "Dudes, what if Zion is also part of the Matrix?"(PS: it's not.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, well what do you know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not the addendum, nor is that quote present at all in the current version of the article, as already indicated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Contrary to any rumors you may have heard, nothing bad happens to D-class personnel at the end of the month. They are actually given cake and ice cream parties and released with a full pardon!"

unprofessional, childish, or silly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, but I still cannot fathom what part of the addendum could be called lolfoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
lolfoundation - N. / ADJ. - A narrative state describing actions on the behalf of or persons affiliated with the SCP Foundation that are, in general, unprofessional, illogical, childish, or silly, generally in the fashion of elderly site articles and tales.

So yeah. It means things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because "lolfoundation" doesn't really mean anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is that a bad thing? The truth functions are obviously not the big reveal here, so I didn't see any point in not being upfront about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One does not simply contain Mordor. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, having it engraved with Veritas pretty much initiates predictability. You hit that line and you know the ring has a truth effect of some sort, and while there's a little wiggle room, it's not far to "so that's what the plot's gonna be".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part you originally complained about has been cleaned up, but I'm honestly not seeing what the issue with the addendum is. That certainly seems to be everyone's favorite part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's pure lolfoundation, and so is the addendum at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm trying to figure out what about that secures your downvote. I see many potential reasons, but nothing stands out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If we need to use an SCP to get D-class to do their jobs, then we're not doing our jobs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you misunderstood — the item was being used to instruct D-class personnel that they would not, in fact, be terminated at the end of the month, but would instead receive a cake and ice cream party. This is obviously not true, hence the need for SCP-1184 to convince them of it.

Clearly I need to rework that part of it, but I think the basic idea (using the item to make D-class personnel more tractable) has merit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is … exactly. Initially downvoted on the gold ring and the casual implication that D-class subjects are not released as the Foundation tells them (still a little dubious this would appear in an SCP report, but I suppose reports frequently contain flat references to "D-XXXX was terminated on schedule", so possibly not as heinous as I initially assumed). Then I got to the bit where you realise that the user is affected too and reversed my vote. The test procedure and followup is genius - and I second Jekeled's comment on the final addendum. Having said that, I do think it needs going through for clinical tone and possibly some of those [DATA EXPUNGED] don't need to be there. Still, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple, easily accepted statements ("Contrary to any rumors you may have heard, nothing bad happens to D-class personnel at the end of the month. They are actually given cake and ice cream parties and released with a full pardon!") are taken at face value without apparent surprise.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to downvote hard, but the addendum saved it from a bad downvote. There a few things like flowery language and [DATA REDACTED] that bother me enough to downvote though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for taking the time to critique this, I really appreciate it. I do disagree about the containment procedures needing to specify non-destructive testing — as all tests must be authorized by The Powers That Be, I personally don't think it matters if every mook reading the report knows what sorts of tests are going to be approved and which are going to be denied. Following the containment procedures as written will result in no destructive testing being performed, because such tests will be denied authorization. I have nonetheless reworded the offending sentence and hopefully it reads better now.

Most of the rest I'm going to leave alone for right now so I can sleep on it. As you say, I don't want to strive so hard for a dry clinical presentation that I lose the overall feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Possibly I overstate the case, but the tone slips are numerous. I'll point out the first couple that jump at me and come back when I have more time for a more thorough list.

-Redacting the approving Docs name in the containment, while not precisely a tone issue, is the first mis-step I see.
-"Only non-destructive testing…" That should be part of the containment, as it deals with what one can/can't do with the item.
-"wholeheartedly", "completely unshakeable ", "simply accepting", "by far" are all less than clinical word choices. Most of the slips were of that nature. I'll get you a more thorough list in that later pass.
-Pretty much all the bits in parentheses read like conversational asides rather than scientific notes.

Now, having defended my inner copy-editor I will shut him in his little cell and lock the door, because if he heard what I am about to say he would ravage my medula. I don't neccesarily recomend fixing these. Good writing overrides poor tone, and you have some very good writing here IMO. If you can patch it up without losing the sparkle, hooray! But don't take chances on spoiling a good thing over some nitpicks.
